meta.region_he: Frontier region · Larkmont (~300 m) · hot, dry summer; cold winter with late frost February–March
meta.source_he: General gardening knowledge — not tailored to the specific variety/rootstock; worth verifying with an agronomist or local experience

Punica granatum.companion_tip_he: Plant mint, lavender or carnation at the edges — they attract pollinators and confuse pests. Keep away from competitive root vegetables and from low trees that shade the canopy.
pollinators: Bees aid fruit set even though the pomegranate is partly self-pollinating
Punica granatum.pollinator_tip_he: To boost fruit set, keep wildflowers and flowering herbs near the tree and do not spray during bee-active hours (morning).

pests.sign_he: Brown, domed shells on branches and leaves, black sooty mold and climbing ants
pests.remedy_he: Manual wiping/brushing, a light mineral-oil spray during dormancy, encouraging ladybugs

pests.sign_he: Entry holes in the fruit crown and internal rot in fruits that blacken and drop
pests.remedy_he: Collect and burn infested fruit, cover the fruit crown or wrap it in a mesh bag, pheromone traps

pests.sign_he: Ripe fruit pecked and split open close to harvest
pests.remedy_he: Shade/protective netting over the tree, reflective deterrent ribbons, timely harvest

month_tasks.task_he: Dormant pruning: remove dry, crossing branches and basal suckers
Finish dormant pruning before bud break; organic fertilizing at the base
Early-spring fertilizing (nitrogen + potassium)
Monitor bud break; protect against late frost on cold nights
Flowering — thin excess flowers to steer toward larger fruit
Increase irrigation as the heat begins
Fruit thinning: thin young fruitlets (leave ~1 per cluster) to prevent splitting
Continue fruit thinning; regular irrigation to prevent skin cracks
Check the drip system ahead of peak summer
Deep, regular irrigation — inconsistent water causes fruit splitting
Maintain steady irrigation; shade exposed fruit from sunburn
Ripening begins — taste and check; harvest when the skin reaches full color
Main harvest; collect before the first rains to prevent splitting
Reduce irrigation ahead of dormancy; plant new saplings during the leaf-fall season
Dormancy — rest; dormant pruning can begin toward its end

Prunus armeniaca.companion_tip_he: Plant garlic, onion or chives at the base to repel aphids, and lavender/mint at the edges. Keep away from nearby peach/plum, which share the same pests and diseases.
pollinators: Bees — essential during the early bloom; cold weather may reduce pollination activity
Prunus armeniaca.pollinator_tip_he: The bloom is early and cold weather reduces bee activity — plant early-flowering anchor plants nearby and avoid spraying during bloom.

pests.sign_he: Curled, shriveled leaves at the shoot tips and secretion of sticky honeydew
pests.remedy_he: A strong jet of water, a potassium-soap solution spray, encouraging ladybugs and parasitic wasps

pests.sign_he: Sting punctures on the fruit and white maggots inside fruit that softens and drops
pests.remedy_he: Pheromone/protein traps, collecting and burning infested fruit, bagging the fruit or netting

pests.sign_he: Branches suddenly drying with blackened blossoms and a gummy stub
pests.remedy_he: Prune and remove infected branches below the damaged area, disinfect pruning tools, prune in the dry season

month_tasks.task_he: Dormant pruning: shape an open canopy, remove inner branches
Finish pruning before bloom; get ready to cover against frost
Early bloom — prepare covering sheets for cold nights
Peak sensitivity to late frost — cover with a sheet on cold nights to save the crop
Thin young fruitlets (~5–8 cm spacing) for large, healthy fruit
Spring fertilizing
Increasing irrigation as the heat approaches; monitor for pests
Harvest begins — apricots ripen early; collect gradually
Finish harvest; light post-harvest pruning to ventilate the canopy
Light summer pruning after harvest
Deep irrigation at peak dryness; mulch to retain moisture
Slightly reduce irrigation; keep the tree healthy ahead of leaf fall
Leaf fall begins; clear fallen leaves from under the tree
Plant bare-root saplings during the leaf-fall season
Dormancy; dormant pruning can begin toward January

Ficus carica.companion_tip_he: Plant rosemary, mint or basil at the base to repel pests. Give the fig room — its roots are aggressive; keep away from sensitive plants that would compete for water.
pollinators: Many home fig varieties bear fruit without pollination (parthenocarpic)
Ficus carica.pollinator_tip_he: A parthenocarpic home variety needs no pollinator — no fig wasp required. It is still worth cultivating flowers around it to boost natural enemies of pests.

pests.sign_he: Fruit that softens, sours and drops, with a sting mark and maggots inside
pests.remedy_he: Frequent harvesting of ripe fruit, collecting and burning dropped fruit, pheromone traps

pests.sign_he: Ripe fruit split open and gnawed, mainly the soft, sweet figs
pests.remedy_he: Timely daily harvesting, mesh bags over clusters, sweet wasp traps placed at a distance

pests.sign_he: Leaves speckled yellow/bronze with fine webbing on the underside in heat and dryness
pests.remedy_he: Raise humidity by misting water on the foliage, neem oil/potassium soap, avoid drought stress

month_tasks.task_he: Dormancy — rest; plan shaping pruning
Dormant pruning: shorten long branches, remove dry ones before bud break
Bud break — organic fertilizing; ensure good drainage in the pot
Increase irrigation gradually; the plant likes slight dryness between waterings
First crop develops (breba); monitor for drying out
Breba harvest begins; steady irrigation for fruit sweetness
Main crop harvest — collect soft figs every day
Continue harvest; protect against birds/wasps as needed
Finish harvest; reduce irrigation gradually
Semi-hardwood cuttings root well — propagation by cuttings is possible
Leaf fall begins; plant new saplings during the leaf-fall season
Dormancy — rest; avoid over-watering

Vaccinium corymbosum.companion_tip_he: Plant only beside acidity-loving plants (azalea, rhododendron) and with pine-needle mulch. Keep away from lime-loving plants that would require an opposing soil amendment.
pollinators: Bees and bumblebees improve fruit set significantly
Vaccinium corymbosum.pollinator_tip_he: Bumblebees (buzz pollination) improve fruit set especially — plant a variety of wildflowers around and do not spray during bloom in order to attract them.

pests.sign_he: Ripe blue berries vanish or are pecked close to ripening
pests.remedy_he: Cover the bush with protective netting before ripening, reflective deterrent ribbons, quick harvesting

pests.sign_he: Colonies on the soft new growth, shriveled leaves and secretion of sticky honeydew
pests.remedy_he: A jet of water, potassium soap, encouraging ladybugs and parasitic wasps

pests.sign_he: General decline, yellowing leaves and brown, soft roots in heavy/waterlogged soil
pests.remedy_he: Excellent drainage and an aerated, acidic medium, avoiding over-watering, planting in a raised bed/pot

month_tasks.task_he: Dormant pruning: remove branches 5+ years old to renew the bush
Finish pruning; check medium acidity (pH 4.5–5.5) and correct with sulfur/acidic medium
Flowering — protect against late frost; do not let the medium dry out
Fruit set — fertilizing for acid-soil plants; irrigation with soft water
Ripening begins; protect against birds with netting
Harvest — collect fully blue berries every few days
Continue harvest; frequent irrigation at peak heat (sensitive to drying out)
Frequent irrigation and shade during the hot midday hours
Refresh acidic mulch; slightly reduce fertilizing ahead of autumn
Plant bare-root young plants in a pot with an acidic mix
Planting is possible; protect from dry winds
Accumulating chill hours for dormancy; reduced irrigation

companions: No competitive plants at the trunk base
Persea americana.companion_tip_he: Keep the trunk base clear of competitive plants; ground cover set slightly back from the trunk is fine for retaining moisture. Keep large trees away that would compete for water.
pollinators: Bees; type A/B flowering — two nearby cultivars improve fruit set
Persea americana.pollinator_tip_he: Type A/B flowering — planting a complementary variety nearby (or having a neighbor with an avocado) improves fruit set. Cultivate wildflowers to attract bees and do not spray during bloom.

pests.sign_he: Brown necrotic spots along the leaf veins and leaf drop in heat and dryness
pests.remedy_he: Rinsing the foliage with water, neem oil/potassium soap, encouraging predatory mites and avoiding drought stress

pests.sign_he: White, cottony shells/clumps on branches and leaves, honeydew and sooty mold
pests.remedy_he: Manual wiping, a light mineral oil; ants should be kept away (they protect the scale insects)

pests.sign_he: Small, yellowing leaves, branch dieback from the tips and slow decline of the tree
pests.remedy_he: Excellent drainage and controlled irrigation (not waterlogged), planting on a mound/raised bed, organic mulch

month_tasks.task_he: Peak frost sensitivity — protect with a wrapped trunk and cover on cold nights
Continue frost protection; avoid fertilizing until the danger passes
End of frost danger — a good time to plant a nursery sapling
Planting after the last frost; moderate spring fertilizing
Spring fertilizing
Flowering/fruit set — regular, consistent irrigation
Steady drip irrigation is essential at peak heat; mulch to retain moisture
High, steady irrigation; shade a young trunk from sunburn
Continue intensive irrigation; monitor for heat stress
Light fertilizing; keep irrigation steady
Prepare winter protection; reduce fertilizing ahead of the cold
Do not plant in autumn in Larkmont — a young tree is vulnerable to the approaching frost
Frost protection: covering, trunk wrapping, a wind-sheltered location

companions: Attracts pollinators and confuses pests — a good neighbor for fig and vegetables
Best in a pot — it spreads in open ground
Mentha spicata.companion_tip_he: An excellent neighbor for fig, fruit trees and leafy vegetables — it confuses pests and attracts pollinators. Grow it in a pot or a sunken container: in open ground it takes over and crowds out neighbors.
pollinators: The summer bloom attracts bees and butterflies — an anchor plant for the garden
Mentha spicata.pollinator_tip_he: Let part of the clump flower in summer — the bloom attracts bees and butterflies and serves as an anchor plant that sustains the whole garden.

pests.sign_he: Green/black colonies on the soft new growth and leaves, sticky and shriveled foliage
pests.remedy_he: A jet of water, picking/cutting back the infested growth, potassium soap, encouraging ladybugs

pests.sign_he: Powdery, rust-orange pustules on the underside of the leaves, which then drop
pests.remedy_he: Remove and destroy infected foliage, prune for ventilation, avoid wetting the foliage and water at the base

pests.sign_he: Yellow speckling on the leaves and fine webbing in summer heat and dryness
pests.remedy_he: Raise humidity by misting water, avoid drought stress, potassium soap/neem oil

month_tasks.task_he: Winter rest — minimal irrigation; protect from hard frost
Prune/clean out dry stems ahead of the awakening
Time to plant/divide roots; light organic fertilizing
Rapid growth — leaf harvest begins; division for propagation
Regular harvest; pinch off flowers to encourage tender foliage
Frequent irrigation — it likes moist soil; light shade at midday
Frequent harvest at peak summer; make sure it does not dry out
Continue irrigation and shade; cut back to encourage new growth
A second time to plant/divide; harvest continues
Divide large clumps; harvest ahead of the cold
Cut back ahead of winter; reduce irrigation
Rest — minimal irrigation; protect from frost
